authoritative_sources.title: NIST Privacy Framework
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST is the U.S. national standards body and the Privacy Framework is a public enterprise privacy-risk management framework for identifying and managing privacy risk across sectors.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: privacy-risk intake, governance context, obligation and control mapping, review cadence

authoritative_sources.title: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST CSF 2.0 is a cross-industry cybersecurity governance and risk-management framework used to align privacy-protective controls with enterprise cyber-risk decisions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: governance context, risk communication, control prioritization, remediation tracking

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-122, Guide to Protecting the Confidentiality of Personally Identifiable Information
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-122 directly addresses identifying PII, confidentiality impact, safeguard selection, and response planning for PII incidents.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: PII identification, classification, confidentiality impact assessment, safeguards, incident preparation

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5, Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 is the public federal control catalog for security and privacy controls and provides traceable evidence and control-mapping expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: control evidence mapping, privacy/security safeguard selection, remediation review

authoritative_sources.title: Protecting Sensitive and Personal Information
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA is the U.S. national cyber defense agency and this resource directly addresses protecting sensitive and personal information from ransomware-caused data breaches.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: ransomware data-exposure preparation, sensitive data protection, breach evidence handling

authoritative_sources.title: #StopRansomware Guide
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA, MS-ISAC, NSA, and FBI authored this operational ransomware guidance; it informs data backup, recovery, and exposure-preparation evidence without replacing incident response execution.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: ransomware resilience review, backup and restore evidence, response handoff

authoritative_sources.title: Protecting Personal Information: A Guide for Business
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The FTC is the U.S. federal consumer-protection regulator for unfair or deceptive data-security practices, and this business guide directly describes personal information inventory, minimization, protection, disposal, and planning workflows.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: data inventory, minimization, access, disposal, incident preparation

authoritative_sources.title: Start with Security: A Guide for Business
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The FTC guide distills practical data-security lessons from enforcement matters and directly informs secure collection, retention, access, service-provider, storage, transmission, and vulnerability-handling workflows.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: privacy-by-design intake, secure handling, service-provider control review, remediation prioritization

authoritative_sources.title: Data Breach Response: A Guide for Business
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The FTC breach response guide describes evidence preservation, legal counsel involvement, affected data assessment, and notification considerations for businesses, constraining this lane to privacy decision support.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: incident-preparation evidence handling, breach decision support, legal and forensics handoff

authoritative_sources.title: Regulation (EU) 2016/679, General Data Protection Regulation
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The GDPR is the primary EU statutory privacy framework defining public obligations and terminology for processing records, processors, security, data protection by design and default, DPIAs, and breach notification.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: regulatory terminology, DPIA support, processor review, breach support, transfer ambiguity escalation

authoritative_sources.title: California Consumer Privacy Act Regulations
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The CPPA is California's privacy regulator and publishes official CCPA/CPRA regulations that define California consumer privacy rights, business obligations, and regulatory terminology.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: consumer privacy obligation mapping, rights-sensitive evidence review, conflict escalation

authoritative_sources.title: CIS Critical Security Control 3: Data Protection
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CIS Controls are a recognized safeguard baseline, and Control 3 directly addresses identifying, classifying, securely handling, retaining, and disposing of data.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: data classification, secure handling, retention, disposal, evidence checklist

authoritative_sources.title: OWASP Application Security Verification Standard
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OWASP ASVS is a recognized application-security verification standard that provides control requirements for applications relied on to protect sensitive data.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: application data protection controls, secure development guidance, vendor/application control review
used_for: boundary between guidance and implementation/testing execution

workflow_stages.name: Intake, boundary, and authority confirmation
workflow_stages.description: Confirm request scope, data owner, system owner, processing purpose, jurisdiction profile, data subject population, authority boundaries, approval matrix, and adjacent owner before producing privacy or data-protection guidance.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Data inventory, classification, and flow mapping
workflow_stages.description: Map data elements, sensitivity, regulated categories, systems, locations, storage, transfers, vendors, logs, backups, retention state, and access paths to tenant systems of record.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Obligation, policy, and control mapping
workflow_stages.description: Normalize public baselines, tenant policy, contract terms, DPA commitments, retention schedules, and supplied legal/privacy obligations into control requirements and caveated recommendations.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Privacy-risk and impact assessment support
workflow_stages.description: Assess purpose limitation, minimization, user impact, data protection by design/default, pseudonymization, anonymization, re-identification risk, processor roles, residual risk, and human review requirements.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Evidence packet and review preparation
workflow_stages.description: Assemble evidence checklists, source freshness, control proof, vendor/processor evidence, deletion or retention logs, access reviews, and traceability for privacy/legal/audit owners without issuing signoff.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Exception, remediation, and incident-preparation escalation
workflow_stages.description: Prioritize remediation, define exception evidence, prepare breach-notification decision support, and escalate conflicts, missing facts, or outside authorities to legal, incident response, architecture, procurement, audit, or orchestrator owners.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

artifacts: data flow map, architecture summary, integration diagram, processing purpose, system owner, data owner, and business owner
data catalog, asset inventory, data element inventory, sensitivity labels, regulated-data categories, storage locations, transfer paths, logs, backups, and exports
tenant data classification policy, privacy policy, retention schedule, deletion standard, incident policy, exception policy, and approval matrix
RoPA, PIA/DPIA record, privacy risk register, data subject rights workflow, consent or preference records, and legal/privacy review notes
GRC control matrix, control evidence packet, access review evidence, KMS or encryption settings, DLP configuration, logging evidence, backup/restore tests, and deletion records
DPA, contract excerpt, subprocessor list, SOC/ISO report, bridge letter, vendor control evidence, data-location statement, deletion/export commitment, and incident notification term
remediation backlog, risk acceptance record, exception register, review cadence, owner assignment, and orchestrator handoff packet
systems_of_record: architecture repository and data-flow diagram store
privacy management platform, RoPA register, PIA/DPIA repository, and data subject rights workflow
GRC/control evidence repository and policy management system
contract lifecycle management and DPA repository
vendor management or third-party risk management platform
records retention, legal hold, deletion, archive, and e-discovery systems
IAM, access review, KMS, secrets, DLP, SIEM, logging, backup, and storage control records
ticketing, remediation backlog, exception register, and risk acceptance register

allowed: classify data and recommend privacy-protective controls by data category, sensitivity, system, owner, purpose, location, and tenant policy tier
prepare obligation-to-control mappings, evidence checklists, privacy impact support, residual-risk rationale, and remediation priorities
review processor or vendor privacy/security evidence and identify DPA, SOC, deletion, subprocessor, and data-location gaps
provide incident-preparation and breach-notification decision support limited to privacy evidence, impacted data, role handoff, and required legal or IR ownership
document assumptions, confidence, missing facts, source freshness, review cadence, and exception routes
escalate: legal basis, statutory interpretation, cross-border transfer mechanism, notification decision, regulator response, counsel signoff, or rights-sensitive final determination
conflicting jurisdiction, tenant policy, contract, regulator guidance, or source-precedence requirements
formal audit attestation, SOC/ISO certification, product compliance badge, or regulator-facing certification
incident-response containment, forensics, eradication, recovery, or breach notification filing
broad architecture, cloud, identity, key-management, platform engineering, procurement approval, vendor risk acceptance, or executive risk decision
refuse: execute penetration testing, exploit validation, payload development, exploit remediation, active probing, red-team activity, or runtime control changes
issue legal opinions, decide legal basis, certify compliance, submit regulator notification, or sign audit reports
approve enterprise architecture, vendor onboarding, DPA execution, data-sharing authorization, procurement action, risk acceptance, or product certification
guess when core data, system, owner, policy, jurisdiction, contract, or evidence facts are missing
treat cross-industry privacy as a generic checklist detached from records, systems, data categories, jurisdictions, contracts, and authority boundaries

domain_failure_modes: A generic privacy checklist is produced without tying recommendations to concrete data categories, systems of record, owners, locations, processing purposes, and authority constraints.
The lane gives a legal interpretation of GDPR, CCPA/CPRA, HIPAA, contract, or data-transfer obligations instead of preparing control-safe options and escalation questions.

Processor or vendor evidence is accepted at face value even though the DPA, SOC scope, subprocessor list, deletion commitment, bridge letter, or data-location evidence is stale, missing, or out of scope.
Anonymized and pseudonymized data are treated as equivalent, causing re-identification risk to be understated.
Retention, deletion, legal hold, finance retention, and data subject rights conflicts are flattened into a single confident recommendation without records/counsel escalation.
Incident-preparation guidance turns into live containment, forensics, notification filing, or root-cause execution.
Control recommendations omit evidence age, owner, source, confidence, review cadence, or exception approval requirement.
Tenant policy that is stricter than the public baseline is weakened because public sources are treated as sufficient.
Platform certification, formal audit attestation, or product compliance claims are made without authorized assurance review.
Source freshness or jurisdictional changes are ignored for high-impact privacy recommendations.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Industry-specific prerequisite or evidence gap
Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
normal in-scope analysis
edge-case analysis with partial evidence
prioritization and recommendation quality
out-of-scope rejection
low-confidence escalation
clear evidence-backed rationale
no unreviewed regressions
Scenario proving output fidelity for: classification matrix and control mapping
Scenario proving output fidelity for: prioritized remediation backlog with effort/risk estimates
Scenario proving output fidelity for: privacy impact assessment and residual-risk rationale
Scenario proving output fidelity for: runbook-style recommendations with evidence references
Scenario proving output fidelity for: scope-limitation notes and escalation guidance
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not perform penetration testing or exploit remediation execution.
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not execute incident-response containment and forensics activities.
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not provide legal opinions or issue formal audit attestation reports.
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not take end-to-end ownership of broad enterprise security architecture decisions.
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not claim platform-level vendor product certification.
Escalation case proving orchestrator return: Return to orchestrator when confidence is low or evidence is insufficient.
Escalation case proving orchestrator return: Return to orchestrator when request crosses specialist boundaries (e.g., penetration testing execution, legal opinion, formal audit attestation).
Escalation case proving orchestrator return: Return to orchestrator when policy conflicts require legal counsel or enterprise counsel sign-off.
Missing or insufficient evidence.
Conflicting policy or jurisdiction requirements.
Request for formal legal interpretation or signoff outside lane authority.
Scenario proving industry-specific in-scope execution.
Scenario proving escalation when industry-specific authority or safety facts are missing.
Scenario proving the lane refuses or escalates when: do not treat the industry name as cosmetic; it must materially change the pack.
Normal in-scope execution using realistic industry terminology and artifact shapes.
Boundary rejection when the request should move to an adjacent specialist.
Low-confidence handling when required facts, records, or authority sources are missing.
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling that requires escalation instead of confident synthesis.
Tenant-specific adaptation handling that preserves the public baseline and records assumptions.

unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The pack does not decide legal basis, transfer legality, notification obligation, or statutory interpretation; those facts must come from tenant policy, counsel, or authorized privacy owner.
Cross-industry use requires concrete industry, data category, customer population, jurisdiction, system, contract, and authority facts; absent facts trigger low-confidence escalation.

Supplier assertions are not treated as independent assurance. The pack labels evidence quality and escalates missing SOC scope, DPA, bridge letter, subprocessor, data-location, deletion, or incident-notification evidence.

notes: No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for a validated specification-based package because legal, audit, certification, incident-response, penetration-testing, architecture, procurement, and runtime-execution authorities are explicitly refused or routed.

Research constrained the final pack to concrete data records, systems of record, evidence quality, source freshness, jurisdiction caveats, and authority boundaries rather than a generic privacy checklist.
